speaker: interviewer
text: Jerry Goose, tape number 2.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Jerry, you were talking about the day that the Russians left Berezne. And shortly--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans walked in.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And you were injured, when the house in which you were staying with your brother, sister, and mother was shot. All right, go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, when I was hurt, it was one of my sister's daughter was hurt-- shrapnel broke up her leg. They took her to the hospital. And she passed away. And then my sister-in-law got hurt. And after three days, she passed away. And a few other people were hurt too. They took him to the hospital. And then we rented a house. It was lots of houses then vacant. Because the Jewish people were not anymore there. They run away a lot. We moved in a house. And then I was living for a while there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, when the Germans came in, it was about maybe six, seven  months. It was hidden everybody quiet, in the place where they was. And once, was in the spring-- I think in August something, they-- they-- they-- they was-- they sent out police and tell the people and everybody in the city-- everybody lives in their houses-- leave the houses the way they are and go up on the marketplace. There was a marketplace. Everybody should just go on the market, not to take anything with them. Then we went to the marketplace and lay there. Before the night came, they told us to go. They put us in lines and took us over to the place where the Polish Army used to be-- barracks. And they made room for them to be stripped over there and stay there for a while. Then later, after three, four-- about two months, There was a city was-- a street-- the worst street in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Berezne.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berezne. I'm sorry, I said London-- Berezne. Then they put the wires around them. And they put in three, four families in one house. Then they arranged a [? malitz, ?] Jewish police. And then there was the Ukrainian police to watch us not to walk out in the street. When you walk out in the street-- yes, and next, they put in yellow patches on us. When they walk out and see you with no yellow patch, you get in jail and maybe get shot. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just interrupt you for a moment. While you were in the barracks--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --how did you survive, if they told you not to bring anything with you? Did you have food? Water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food, they give us. They brought us over food and water, everything there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they moved you into the ghetto. It was just one street.

speaker: interviewee
text: One street. The whole city, where all the Jewish people was.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people do you think were in that one street?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think about-- I don't know. About 2,000. It was-- was about 2,000 people. Well, they put in three or four families in one, little room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you living with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was living with my parents. There was a garage there. I was living in a garage.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who else was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, my father, mother, and a sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was your brother, Kieva?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kieva, I don't remember where he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Gottel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember where he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there was you, your father, and mother, and a sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe they got another place there. Because they used to have a house for himself, Kieva. Maybe he went with Gottel in another place. I don't remember, I never saw him. I really didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see them again after you went into the ghetto, your two brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't see them while you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Because I got no time. Like I tell you the story before. I never liked to sleep in the ghetto. I know it was going to happen sometime. I always tell the police, give me a place to work out from the ghetto. And then they permitted me to work in-- 20 miles out from the city was a station, where the train comes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mokwin, Station Mokwin. There was a station in Mokwin. And then they gave me a job to work on the rails-- to put the rails for the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were building railroads?

speaker: interviewee
text: Building railroads. And between time, they rented us a little place. We were about six or seven people. It was let Monday morning and back Saturday night home.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about life in the ghetto, that you remember, even though you weren't there very much?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto, just like that, they used to take any people that want to work. They used to line them up and take up the field and working by fields or make some like heating buildings with grass-- you know, the dry grass-- cut it up and they dry them up and heat the stoves, you know, in the place for coal. Some groups went there. And some, they gave them other jobs. But I never went there. Because I said I wanted to be there, far away. And I got my job steady.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your parents survive in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: They not survive-- how--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, until before they died, how did they manage to get food to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's what I want to tell you, about the food, again when you asked me. When I went work to the station-- there to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mokwin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mokwin. Then my parents gave me some pants. The other one gave me a dress-- the mother or my sister gave me dress. And walking through 20 miles, I went to the farmer and said, how much you're going to give me bread or salt or something? Then they promised me they gave me a half a loaf of bread. They gave me a loaf  a bread. And when Saturday night I came back to the place, then I brought them over the bread with everybody. In between time, it was every morning coffee and soup for lunch. And in the evenings, I don't know, something give them to eat. And that's how they lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then was happened another story with me they come. The Germans said they needed some people. They needed horses to send to Russia. And they gave an order to the farmers to get how many horses they got, to deliver the horses in one place. And the farmer brought over the horses. And they picked people to drive the horses. And this was a city of Dubno far away, maybe 200 miles. Then they picked me. I never saw-- I saw a horse, I never drive horses. They gave me three horses and a buggy. And then between breaks, I got not easy And they told me to come to Dubno in the morning. Then when I came-- the morning, there came the SS. And they gave us an order to take the horses and put them in the train-- on the train to-- They wanted to deliver them to the-- to-- to the-- Russia. And the place to make the horses run and was-- I was-- when I [INAUDIBLE] with the horses, [INAUDIBLE] was another friend with me, in my city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we work both at the same buggy, driving the horses. Then after loading the horses on the train, the SS with a stick at the place to make the horses, tempo, tempo, they started beating us. And we couldn't take anymore. I couldn't stand anymore. I told them, you know what? Let go-- I bet you in the city is a Judenrat. You know what a  Judenrat mean. And we go up to the city and run away. And then we'll see later what's going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which city are you talking about now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dubno.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I, when we came to the-- when I started walking to the city, we run away. . The SS never say-- [? those ?] [? sounds. ?] Just we forgot that Jewish people are not permitted to walk on the sidewalk. And when you get a Jewish person walk on the sidewalk, then they kill. They don't leave them alive. And I walked beside the sidewalk with the other guy. And then comes a Ukrainian policeman. [INAUDIBLE]. The Ukrainians killed 40% Jewish people and 60% the Germans. Then they brought me over to their station for the Ukrainian police. And they start-- and it was dark in the room. They just started beating us. In the eyes, it was just a little light was coming-- with fists, with everything. Then, and let us-- I slept on the floor to the morning. In the morning, they call out to the police, to the German. And the Germans telled the Ukrainian police to bring him over to their office, to the German's office. When we came to the German's office, then the Germans started asking me from where I come and what's what, and what's what. And then later, what they did-- [INAUDIBLE] in-- in this building, they've got a basement like a jail in a basement. And they tell us to go in the basement until father is going to call you. We came in the basement. There was already about one Jew there and about six, seven Ukrainish people. And I-- and I saw in a minute they started making fun with the Jew. He should dance. And they throw him to one, go to the other one, so to the other one. And they danced them. And they'd laughed at the Jew. And I said to my friends, I think we should-- maybe you got some foods. You got something. Let's right away make friends with them. Were not, then we're going to have the same trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I speak Ukrainian too. And tell them I got food, and I got this. And you want to-- and you want we should dance? We could dance right away to not to make fun later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they accept the bread. And they saw we are not the same like the other guy. And they start telling us. They say, listen, when you see the locks start-- they open the lock in the middle of the night, then they come in and take out people and get them to dead. And when they, when you hear they're coming in, the Germans, where you sleep-- you sleep with one wooden bed, a long one, and everybody sleeps there-- you have to right away [INAUDIBLE] and stay straight. You know, go down to the bed to stay straight until they are walking out. Well, the second night, we heard they are coming. Right away, everybody stayed straight. And they said to the Jew, you come. And another somebody they took him. They took him away, I didn't see him anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then later, I was [INAUDIBLE] happy to see Jewish people. There was-- there was a Jewish ghetto there too. To see somebody, maybe they could take a pass. And again, they said are you Ukrainian? They said they were anti-Semitic. They said, you know what? Next time you go.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, when I have to go, I tell them I have to go. And then every time, when there was a little window to the-- to the jail downstairs and Jewish people run through-- I could see because they got the yellow thing. But if they don't run, then look, it's because they're afraid. One day, the Ukrainish people used to make fun for me. They used to say in Ukrainish. [SPEAKING UKRAINIAN]. To them, it's a martyr-- Ukraine, you know, German. And for Jewish people, it's a war-- a translation in-- in this in English, what they say in Russia. Well, then later in-- a week later, again, they come for the Ukrainish people, picked them up too. They murdered them or something. And they took  the two people. And I tell them the same in Ukrainian. [SPEAKING  UKRAINIAN]. They took them away too. Well, that's then. Later on, another week later, I heard through the day they brought in another Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your friend were still there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was still there, with the friend. Between us, both was. And they brought in another Jew. And I asked him, what is the reason you are here? And I was feeling better, because this Jew is from the same town.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Berezne.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. From this-- I forgot what-- I told you before the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do-- do-- I can't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what I mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the same city, Dubno. In the same city. Then I felt bad because as soon, I know, they find out in the ghetto he's there, they are going after him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then I asked him, why are you there? He said, I was working for a German, fixing the [? hydro. ?] And I forgot I had to wear the jacket with the patch. And I put it on and my arm was hot.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they saw me walking with no patch-- the Ukrainian police. They brought him over the German. And for them, you know what he get from that, the same thing. Well later, a day later, I see already through the window, there  are no Jewish people. And this-- And  they made me [INAUDIBLE] to me. Then I said [INAUDIBLE] got, I got somebody going to take me out. In this [INAUDIBLE] a big [? gun. ?] Then there comes two Germans and tell us, you come with me. This means these Germans, they are not SS-- the Wehr people. They look after the horses. They look after-- They're nicer Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said, you come with us. They got a buggy with horses. And they told us to sit down on the buggy. OK, you want me to tell you the story? Sit  down on the buggy. And you come with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we go on in a country some place. And I see a wood. I tell to the guy, I think there they're going to fix us up and leave us there. And I don't say myself, I just said my friend was black. Everything was black. He could not talk, because he was so afraid. And I was the same. But I didn't sound like he was. Well, I'll make the story short. They brought us over in a farm with some farmer's hay for the horses. And  they told us to put in the buggy, the big buggy, made us fill it up with hay. My boy come-- his father was a doctor. This friend mine. He came from a home where they never [? saw ?] horses. And I never-- I saw hay. I never put that-- they said put it in the buggy. I don't how to put it in a tall buggy with hay. You know, after the big ones. We put in the hay. And they brought us back to them, to where they took us and put them back in jail. In this jail. Then another day, they called us again the same time. Then it was already Jewish people waiting for us and gave us bread and gave us another thing and said, don't worry. We're going to take you out. And a day later, again, the two Germans brought us. The Germans were nice people. They said, you know, the schweine-- You know what a schweine.

words.text: We're going to take you out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Schweine, yeah, pigs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't tell the schweine. Don't tell the Ukrainian people. You'll be another day, and then they'll let you go. I was ready to kiss the Germans. But don't tell them nothing. Now, the next day, they come. And they came from the Judenrat and took out-- we are two and [INAUDIBLE]. They took us out. Then we have to go back to home.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Berezne.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Berezne. And to Berezne is about 100 miles or some more. Then we came and we were walking to Rivne-- to Rivne. I mentioned to you Rivne. That from Rivne is another 70 miles to Berezne. When we are coming close to Rivne-- the city is in a ring. And they started murdering  all the Jewish people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They ringed around. And they started putting the people in lines and put them out. And there who helped them, the Ukrainian people. It was not too many Germans. It was more Ukrainian people. Then I had been already in the ring. I couldn't do nothing. The Ukrainian people, you have to come with us. The Germans gave me  a letter, and I drive the horses. When you come there, you should show who you are. And then, they could let you go home. When they stopped me, they wanted to take me with them. I say I got a [? shine. ?] I got a paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Schutz-pass.

speaker: interviewee
text: He couldn't read it. Because there are people, Ukrainian people, who could hardly read. But he saw this with the German, they said, OK, you could go. Well, we ran fast out from the city. Then was a-- a garbage truck-- not a-- gravel truck. And we were begging the guy to a let them go. And we have to go from Rivne to Kostopil. And then I drive to Kostopil. We came close to Kostopil. The other city is in a ring again. Then I ran it again further. Then we ran to a farmer. And he tells us, stay-- no, then, I'm sorry, from then, I went-- I went to home to Berezne. And my parents still were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the sister, yes. And then just a little story once.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: You want farther now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go on. Then what happened? You went back to Berezne--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: --and your parents were there and your sister was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was the same. Low on food. I don't brought them any more bread because you know that. And then I went back to working at the same job. When I came at the same job, between work and the job, I picked together-- farmers was owing me bread. They give me the bread. And I thought in the morning, I think they got lots of bread. I go on back to Berezne and give my parents the bread with everything. I walked out three miles from the village, comes over a Ukrainian. He knows me, because I used to come and give him clothing for bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, where are you going? I said, what do you mean where am I going? I'm going home, you know that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't go home. The whole city is in a ring. And they are killing all the city, the whole town. [INAUDIBLE] Today they are going to be all killed. And they started-- I heard the guns shooting, shooting gun noise-- the guns. Then I thought, I don't go anymore to Berezne. There is no use. They said, if you want to be killed, go. Then I knew a farmer. What they used to always tell me, when you got trouble, and the Germans come over, and I'm going to support you and let you  sleep  a few days, till you find another place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember any name. Because you see, after that-- just then he told me, he said, no, I couldn't tell you and keep you in my house. Just here is a bush. Go in the bush and hide in the bush for the time being. OK, I went to the bush. In the middle of the night, I heard coming some more people. This was a run away from Kostopil, the people who were in a ring. They run-- a lot was run away by seven, eight people.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you talk about in a ring, what do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, the Jews with police, they took them together in one place. They don't let them out already. That's what I call a ring.

speaker: interviewer
text: A circle.

speaker: interviewee
text: A circle, let's say. Sorry, maybe I said the wrong way-- in a circle. Then it was already about 27 people came together to this bush. And there was in the bush two before Rosh Hashanah, before Yom Kippur, when it started getting cold And farmers started-- you want to add more?

speaker: interviewer
text: Just finish this.

speaker: interviewee
text: The farmers started threatening us. They say, we used to stay the night, digging potatoes from the farmers and eating through the day potatoes. Because the farmers tell us, if you're going to again steal potatoes, we'll call the Germans on you. And then we never stole them. And we make a little meeting between us. And we were supposed to go from this place. In about 30 miles, it started big, long bushes-- about 100 miles bushes, tall ones-- not bushes, woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: A forest, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Woods. Then we had to cross water. And we had to go to our village. In this village is-- is Ukrainian people. They called them Banderovci. They were nationalistic Ukrainians. They were fighting for Ukraine. They used to-- They killed Jews, they killed the Polish people. Because they fight-- they want Ukraine for itself. This day it was not-- it was Russian, not Ukraine, not Ukraine. Then [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop for now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
